title: Syncope — Approach
oneLiner: Transient loss of consciousness due to cerebral hypoperfusion; common in all ages, but cardiac syncope carries sudden death risk — rule out life-threatening causes first.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Cardiac syncope (arrhythmic):** Ventricular tachycardia / complete heart block -> pulseless arrest. *Finding:* ECG shows wide-QRS tachycardia, prolonged QT, or high-grade AV block (Mobitz II / complete heart block with slow ventricular rate).
- **Pulmonary embolism (massive):** Obstructive shock +/- syncope as presenting symptom. *Finding:* Haemodynamically unstable (systolic BP <90, tachycardia), raised JVP, and hypoxia (SpO₂ <94% on room air) — ask about recent immobility/surgery. ECG may show S1Q3T3, but normal ECG does not rule out.
- **Tamponade:** Beck’s triad (hypotension, muffled heart sounds, distended neck veins) + pulsus paradoxus. *Finding:* Distended neck veins despite hypotension.
- **Haemorrhage (e.g. ruptured AAA, GI bleed):** Syncope with abdominal/back pain, hypotension, and peritoneal signs. *Finding:* Distended, tender abdomen + shock.
sections.Presentation:
- Cardinal symptoms:
  - Sudden transient loss of consciousness (seconds to minutes)
  - Rapid, spontaneous recovery — no post-ictal confusion (though brief confusion can occur in convulsive syncope)
  - May be preceded by prodrome: lightheadedness, nausea, sweating, visual blurring (vasovagal) OR no prodrome (cardiac/arrhythmic)
  - Witnessed episode: pallor, limpness, brief jerking (convulsive syncope differentiates from seizure by short duration <30 sec, no tongue bite, no prolonged confusion)
- Key exam findings:
  - Vital signs: orthostatic hypotension (drop ≥20 mmHg systolic or ≥10 mmHg diastolic upon standing) -> suggests volume depletion or autonomic failure
  - Cardiac exam: irregularly irregular pulse (AF), murmur (aortic stenosis), signs of heart failure (elevated JVP, crackles, oedema)
  - Carotid sinus massage (if no contraindication: no carotid bruit, no recent stroke/TIA, no MI) — reproduce symptoms with asystole >3 sec -> carotid sinus hypersensitivity
  - Neurological: focal deficits? (suggests TIA/stroke rather than syncope)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Vasovagal syncope (neurocardiogenic):** most common; triggers (pain, fear, prolonged standing); prodrome; rapid recovery; no cardiac abnormality. *Splitter:* prodrome present + normal ECG → likely vasovagal; no prodrome + abnormal ECG → cardiac until proven otherwise.
- **Orthostatic hypotension:** drop in BP upon standing; causes: dehydration, medications (diuretics, alpha-blockers), autonomic neuropathy (DM, Parkinson’s). *Bedside test:* lying/standing BP.
- **Cardiac syncope (arrhythmic):** no prodrome or brief palpitations; ECG: long QT, Brugada, WPW, heart block, VT. *Key:* family history of sudden death <50 years.
- **Cardiac syncope (structural):** aortic stenosis (exertional syncope, systolic murmur), hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, atrial myxoma.
- **Seizure:** tongue biting, post-ictal confusion, prolonged tonic-clonic movements, automatisms. *Differentiator:* convulsive syncope (brief jerks <30 sec) vs true seizure (>1 min, lateral tongue bite, confusion).
- **Psychogenic pseudosyncope:** normal physiology; can be frequent, no injury; witnessed episode with eyes closed, no change in HR/BP.
- **Rare but dangerous:** subarachnoid haemorrhage (thunderclap headache, neck stiffness), pulmonary embolism, tamponade, haemorrhage (see Killers First).
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - 12-lead ECG — mandatory (can be normal in paroxysmal arrhythmia)
  - Orthostatic BP (if able to stand safely) — measure at 1 and 3 minutes
  - Capillary blood glucose (exclude hypoglycaemia)
  - Pulse oximetry + urine pregnancy test (in women of childbearing age)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, glucose, troponin (if cardiac suspicion)
  - Consider D-dimer (if low/intermediate pre-test probability for PE) — beware false positive in elderly, admitted patients
  - BNP/NT-proBNP if heart failure suspected
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: none routinely — only if specific suspicion (e.g. CT head if trauma/headache/anticoagulation; CT pulmonary angiogram if high-risk PE)
  - Second-line: echo (transthoracic) if abnormal ECG/exam suggests structural heart disease
- **Special tests:**
  - Tilt-table testing: if unexplained recurrent syncope and low risk
  - Holter monitor / event loop recorder (for recurrent unexplained syncope) — consider if >1 episode in a year, low risk, to capture arrhythmia
  - Carotid sinus massage (see above) — if age >40 and unexplained syncope with no contraindications
  - Electrophysiology study rarely first-line (guided by cardiology)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **Canadian Syncope Risk Score:** 0-3 low risk (discharge), 4-5 intermediate (observation), 6+ high risk (admit); components: predisposing vasovagal? (no = +1), heart disease (structural or ECG abnormality = +1), systolic BP <90 in ED (+2), elevated troponin (+2), QTc >500ms (+2), diagnosis of vasovagal (low risk = -2). Use only to risk-stratify; not a replacement for clinical judgement.
- **Never-miss test:** 12-lead ECG — omission is indefensible. Second never-miss: pregnancy test in any woman of childbearing age.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Secure airway if consciousness not returning rapidly; ABCDE approach
  - If arrhythmia on monitor: treat per ALS algorithms (e.g. synchronised cardioversion for unstable VT, atropine/transcutaneous pacing for bradycardia)
  - IV fluids if signs of hypovolaemia (crystalloid bolus 500 mL)
  - Treat hypoglycaemia (50 mL 50% dextrose IV or 1 mg glucagon IM)
  - If orthostatic hypotension: remove offending meds, encourage salt/fluid, consider compression stockings
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Admit if: cardiac syncope suspected, high-risk features (age >65, structural heart disease, abnormal ECG, family history sudden death, trauma from syncope, recurrent syncope)
  - Telemetry monitoring (at least 24h) — captures arrhythmias
  - Further investigations (echo, loop recorder) as indicated
  - Fall risk assessment: mobilise with assistance, bed rails, call bell within reach
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Recurrent vasovagal: education to avoid triggers, physical counter-pressure manoeuvres (leg crossing, squatting), increased salt/fluid intake; consider midodrine (if severe) — [check local policy for dosing]
  - Orthostatic hypotension: review medications, compression stockings ± fludrocortisone (50-100 mcg daily, titrated; monitor for supine hypertension)
  - Cardiac syncope: treat underlying cause (e.g. anti-arrhythmics, pacing, implantable cardioverter-defibrillator)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 1L 0.9% sodium chloride IV over 8h if hypotenised (or oral fluids encouraged if tolerated)
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (if not at high bleed risk, e.g. recent stroke, coagulopathy)
  - Diet: normal as tolerated; encourage salt (unless contraindicated e.g. heart failure)
  - Observations: NEWS2 every 1h for first 4h, then 4-hourly if stable; cardiac telemetry (continuous ECG monitoring) for at least 24h
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Fludrocortisone** (for neurogenic orthostatic hypotension): start 50-100 mcg PO once daily; maximum 200 mcg daily. Monitor for supine hypertension, hypokalaemia (check U&E weekly). *Contraindications:* congestive heart failure, severe renal impairment, steroid psychosis. *Interactions:* enhances effect of antihypertensives (can worsen orthostasis paradoxically).
- **Midodrine** (if fludrocortisone fails, or for severe orthostatic hypotension): 2.5-10 mg PO three times daily (avoid after 6 pm). *Contraindications:* severe hypertension, urinary retention, thyrotoxicosis. *Interaction:* caution with other vasoconstrictors.
- **Beta-blockers** (for certain vasovagal, but weak evidence — usually not first-line; cardiology guidance required).
- **Key contraindication:** stop beta-blocker in acute syncope if bradycardia/hypotension — worsens haemodynamics.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** fludrocortisone no adjustment; midodrine reduce dose in liver impairment; enoxaparin adjusted for CrCl <30 mL/min (use prophylactic dose cautiously, consider monitoring anti-Xa).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Giving beta-blocker to a patient with unrecognised complete heart block — can cause asystole. Always check ECG before starting any rate-slowing medication in syncope.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** Trauma (falls, head injury, fractures), aspiration if vomiting, cardiac arrest (from arrhythmic syncope), recurrent falls leading to subdural haematoma in elderly.
- **Chronic:** Psychological distress (fear of recurrence), activity restriction, social isolation, recurrent admissions.
sections.Prognosis:
- Vasovagal syncope: excellent prognosis — not increased mortality, but quality of life can be impaired.
- Cardiac syncope: 1-year mortality up to 30% (underlying heart disease). Risk stratification essential. Orthostatic hypotension: associated with falls, fractures, and increased cardiovascular mortality (due to underlying condition).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always perform an ECG immediately after syncope** — even if patient has recovered. A normal ECG reduces but does not eliminate cardiac cause.
- **Ask about witness** — describe the episode. Brief jerking <30 sec is convulsive syncope, not epilepsy; prolonged confusion >5 min points to seizure.
- **Use orthostatic BP in all elderly syncope patients** — it is the most common and treatable cause (often meds: diuretics, alpha-blockers, antidepressants).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not label a patient with “simple faint” without checking ECG and orthostatics** — missing cardiac syncope is a preventable death.
- **Do not order CT head routinely** — only if head injury, focal neurology, or on anticoagulation. CT without indication exposes to radiation and delays proper workup.
- **Do not ignore medication history** — new prescriptions (e.g. tamsulosin, nitrates, diuretics) are common culprits. Always review the drug chart.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Recurrent syncope, chest pain/palpitations, severe headache (SAH), trauma from fall (head injury, abdominal injury), new weakness or confusion.
- **Follow-up:** GP within 1 week (review meds, check orthostatics); if suspect cardiac syncope — cardiology referral within 2 weeks (urgent if high-risk features). If unexplained — consider loop recorder referral.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / crash team:** GCS <8, airway compromise, persistent hypotension despite fluids, malignant arrhythmia (VT, VF, complete heart block with bradycardia <40), NEWS2 ≥7.
- **Specific thresholds:** Systolic BP <90 mmHg (and not responding to 500 mL bolus), lactate >4 mmol/L, SpO₂ <92% despite O₂, GCS <13 (sustained), chest pain with ECG changes (STEMI call).
- **ICU referral:** Haemodynamic instability requiring inotropes, recurrent syncope due to arrhythmia not controlled, pacemaker dependency.

rawMarkdown:
# Syncope — Approach
**One-liner:** Transient loss of consciousness due to cerebral hypoperfusion; common in all ages, but cardiac syncope carries sudden death risk — rule out life-threatening causes first.

## Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Cardiac syncope (arrhythmic):** Ventricular tachycardia / complete heart block -> pulseless arrest. *Finding:* ECG shows wide-QRS tachycardia, prolonged QT, or high-grade AV block (Mobitz II / complete heart block with slow ventricular rate).
- **Pulmonary embolism (massive):** Obstructive shock +/- syncope as presenting symptom. *Finding:* Haemodynamically unstable (systolic BP <90, tachycardia), raised JVP, and hypoxia (SpO₂ <94% on room air) — ask about recent immobility/surgery. ECG may show S1Q3T3, but normal ECG does not rule out.
- **Tamponade:** Beck’s triad (hypotension, muffled heart sounds, distended neck veins) + pulsus paradoxus. *Finding:* Distended neck veins despite hypotension.
- **Haemorrhage (e.g. ruptured AAA, GI bleed):** Syncope with abdominal/back pain, hypotension, and peritoneal signs. *Finding:* Distended, tender abdomen + shock.

## Presentation
- Cardinal symptoms:
  - Sudden transient loss of consciousness (seconds to minutes)
  - Rapid, spontaneous recovery — no post-ictal confusion (though brief confusion can occur in convulsive syncope)
  - May be preceded by prodrome: lightheadedness, nausea, sweating, visual blurring (vasovagal) OR no prodrome (cardiac/arrhythmic)
  - Witnessed episode: pallor, limpness, brief jerking (convulsive syncope differentiates from seizure by short duration <30 sec, no tongue bite, no prolonged confusion)
- Key exam findings:
  - Vital signs: orthostatic hypotension (drop ≥20 mmHg systolic or ≥10 mmHg diastolic upon standing) -> suggests volume depletion or autonomic failure
  - Cardiac exam: irregularly irregular pulse (AF), murmur (aortic stenosis), signs of heart failure (elevated JVP, crackles, oedema)
  - Carotid sinus massage (if no contraindication: no carotid bruit, no recent stroke/TIA, no MI) — reproduce symptoms with asystole >3 sec -> carotid sinus hypersensitivity
  - Neurological: focal deficits? (suggests TIA/stroke rather than syncope)

## Differential Diagnosis
- **Vasovagal syncope (neurocardiogenic):** most common; triggers (pain, fear, prolonged standing); prodrome; rapid recovery; no cardiac abnormality. *Splitter:* prodrome present + normal ECG → likely vasovagal; no prodrome + abnormal ECG → cardiac until proven otherwise.
- **Orthostatic hypotension:** drop in BP upon standing; causes: dehydration, medications (diuretics, alpha-blockers), autonomic neuropathy (DM, Parkinson’s). *Bedside test:* lying/standing BP.
- **Cardiac syncope (arrhythmic):** no prodrome or brief palpitations; ECG: long QT, Brugada, WPW, heart block, VT. *Key:* family history of sudden death <50 years.
- **Cardiac syncope (structural):** aortic stenosis (exertional syncope, systolic murmur), hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, atrial myxoma.
- **Seizure:** tongue biting, post-ictal confusion, prolonged tonic-clonic movements, automatisms. *Differentiator:* convulsive syncope (brief jerks <30 sec) vs true seizure (>1 min, lateral tongue bite, confusion).
- **Psychogenic pseudosyncope:** normal physiology; can be frequent, no injury; witnessed episode with eyes closed, no change in HR/BP.
- **Rare but dangerous:** subarachnoid haemorrhage (thunderclap headache, neck stiffness), pulmonary embolism, tamponade, haemorrhage (see Killers First).

## Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - 12-lead ECG — mandatory (can be normal in paroxysmal arrhythmia)
  - Orthostatic BP (if able to stand safely) — measure at 1 and 3 minutes
  - Capillary blood glucose (exclude hypoglycaemia)
  - Pulse oximetry + urine pregnancy test (in women of childbearing age)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, glucose, troponin (if cardiac suspicion)
  - Consider D-dimer (if low/intermediate pre-test probability for PE) — beware false positive in elderly, admitted patients
  - BNP/NT-proBNP if heart failure suspected
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: none routinely — only if specific suspicion (e.g. CT head if trauma/headache/anticoagulation; CT pulmonary angiogram if high-risk PE)
  - Second-line: echo (transthoracic) if abnormal ECG/exam suggests structural heart disease
- **Special tests:**
  - Tilt-table testing: if unexplained recurrent syncope and low risk
  - Holter monitor / event loop recorder (for recurrent unexplained syncope) — consider if >1 episode in a year, low risk, to capture arrhythmia
  - Carotid sinus massage (see above) — if age >40 and unexplained syncope with no contraindications
  - Electrophysiology study rarely first-line (guided by cardiology)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **Canadian Syncope Risk Score:** 0-3 low risk (discharge), 4-5 intermediate (observation), 6+ high risk (admit); components: predisposing vasovagal? (no = +1), heart disease (structural or ECG abnormality = +1), systolic BP <90 in ED (+2), elevated troponin (+2), QTc >500ms (+2), diagnosis of vasovagal (low risk = -2). Use only to risk-stratify; not a replacement for clinical judgement.
- **Never-miss test:** 12-lead ECG — omission is indefensible. Second never-miss: pregnancy test in any woman of childbearing age.

## Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Secure airway if consciousness not returning rapidly; ABCDE approach
  - If arrhythmia on monitor: treat per ALS algorithms (e.g. synchronised cardioversion for unstable VT, atropine/transcutaneous pacing for bradycardia)
  - IV fluids if signs of hypovolaemia (crystalloid bolus 500 mL)
  - Treat hypoglycaemia (50 mL 50% dextrose IV or 1 mg glucagon IM)
  - If orthostatic hypotension: remove offending meds, encourage salt/fluid, consider compression stockings
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Admit if: cardiac syncope suspected, high-risk features (age >65, structural heart disease, abnormal ECG, family history sudden death, trauma from syncope, recurrent syncope)
  - Telemetry monitoring (at least 24h) — captures arrhythmias
  - Further investigations (echo, loop recorder) as indicated
  - Fall risk assessment: mobilise with assistance, bed rails, call bell within reach
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Recurrent vasovagal: education to avoid triggers, physical counter-pressure manoeuvres (leg crossing, squatting), increased salt/fluid intake; consider midodrine (if severe) — [check local policy for dosing]
  - Orthostatic hypotension: review medications, compression stockings ± fludrocortisone (50-100 mcg daily, titrated; monitor for supine hypertension)
  - Cardiac syncope: treat underlying cause (e.g. anti-arrhythmics, pacing, implantable cardioverter-defibrillator)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 1L 0.9% sodium chloride IV over 8h if hypotenised (or oral fluids encouraged if tolerated)
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (if not at high bleed risk, e.g. recent stroke, coagulopathy)
  - Diet: normal as tolerated; encourage salt (unless contraindicated e.g. heart failure)
  - Observations: NEWS2 every 1h for first 4h, then 4-hourly if stable; cardiac telemetry (continuous ECG monitoring) for at least 24h

## Drugs & Doses
- **Fludrocortisone** (for neurogenic orthostatic hypotension): start 50-100 mcg PO once daily; maximum 200 mcg daily. Monitor for supine hypertension, hypokalaemia (check U&E weekly). *Contraindications:* congestive heart failure, severe renal impairment, steroid psychosis. *Interactions:* enhances effect of antihypertensives (can worsen orthostasis paradoxically).
- **Midodrine** (if fludrocortisone fails, or for severe orthostatic hypotension): 2.5-10 mg PO three times daily (avoid after 6 pm). *Contraindications:* severe hypertension, urinary retention, thyrotoxicosis. *Interaction:* caution with other vasoconstrictors.
- **Beta-blockers** (for certain vasovagal, but weak evidence — usually not first-line; cardiology guidance required).
- **Key contraindication:** stop beta-blocker in acute syncope if bradycardia/hypotension — worsens haemodynamics.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** fludrocortisone no adjustment; midodrine reduce dose in liver impairment; enoxaparin adjusted for CrCl <30 mL/min (use prophylactic dose cautiously, consider monitoring anti-Xa).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Giving beta-blocker to a patient with unrecognised complete heart block — can cause asystole. Always check ECG before starting any rate-slowing medication in syncope.

## Complications
- **Acute:** Trauma (falls, head injury, fractures), aspiration if vomiting, cardiac arrest (from arrhythmic syncope), recurrent falls leading to subdural haematoma in elderly.
- **Chronic:** Psychological distress (fear of recurrence), activity restriction, social isolation, recurrent admissions.

## Prognosis
- Vasovagal syncope: excellent prognosis — not increased mortality, but quality of life can be impaired.
- Cardiac syncope: 1-year mortality up to 30% (underlying heart disease). Risk stratification essential. Orthostatic hypotension: associated with falls, fractures, and increased cardiovascular mortality (due to underlying condition).

## Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always perform an ECG immediately after syncope** — even if patient has recovered. A normal ECG reduces but does not eliminate cardiac cause.
- **Ask about witness** — describe the episode. Brief jerking <30 sec is convulsive syncope, not epilepsy; prolonged confusion >5 min points to seizure.
- **Use orthostatic BP in all elderly syncope patients** — it is the most common and treatable cause (often meds: diuretics, alpha-blockers, antidepressants).

## Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not label a patient with “simple faint” without checking ECG and orthostatics** — missing cardiac syncope is a preventable death.
- **Do not order CT head routinely** — only if head injury, focal neurology, or on anticoagulation. CT without indication exposes to radiation and delays proper workup.
- **Do not ignore medication history** — new prescriptions (e.g. tamsulosin, nitrates, diuretics) are common culprits. Always review the drug chart.

## Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Recurrent syncope, chest pain/palpitations, severe headache (SAH), trauma from fall (head injury, abdominal injury), new weakness or confusion.
- **Follow-up:** GP within 1 week (review meds, check orthostatics); if suspect cardiac syncope — cardiology referral within 2 weeks (urgent if high-risk features). If unexplained — consider loop recorder referral.

## Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / crash team:** GCS <8, airway compromise, persistent hypotension despite fluids, malignant arrhythmia (VT, VF, complete heart block with bradycardia <40), NEWS2 ≥7.
- **Specific thresholds:** Systolic BP <90 mmHg (and not responding to 500 mL bolus), lactate >4 mmol/L, SpO₂ <92% despite O₂, GCS <13 (sustained), chest pain with ECG changes (STEMI call).
- **ICU referral:** Haemodynamic instability requiring inotropes, recurrent syncope due to arrhythmia not controlled, pacemaker dependency.
